topicArea: Environmental Thought, the Idea of Wilderness & the Frame Around the Photograph

questions.passage:
The photograph is familiar from calendars and lobby walls: a valley at dawn, mist on the river, forest unbroken to the ridgeline, and no human trace anywhere in the frame. The caption says untouched. The valley pictured, like most such valleys, was inhabited, hunted, burned, and cultivated for thousands of years before the photographer arrived; the open meadow in the foreground that reads as nature's own composition was in all likelihood maintained by generations of deliberate fire. What the photograph records is not the absence of people but their removal, from the land in one century and from the frame in another, and the caption quietly converts that double removal into a fact about the valley itself.

The idea doing the converting has a history shorter than most of its admirers assume. For most of the recorded past, the word wilderness and its cognates named something dreaded, waste, desolation, the place of exile and trial, land that was, in the ledger of settlement, worthless until improved. The reversal came late and quickly: within roughly a century, the same landscapes were re-described as cathedrals, as the last remnants of an original creation, as the places where a person went not to be punished but to be restored. Two currents fed the change, a theology of the sublime that relocated the divine from churches to peaks and chasms, and a national romance that recast the receding frontier as the crucible of character. Wilderness did not shed its religious charge in this reversal; the charge simply switched poles, and the dread became reverence.

What the new reverence required, its critics have since documented, was a particular kind of emptiness. Land that was to stand as original creation could show no signs of history, and where inhabitants and their signs existed, they were removed, sometimes by treaty, sometimes by force, and their traces allowed to fade into what visitors would learn to see as pristine. The baseline against which later change would be measured as degradation was thus not a state of nature but a snapshot, taken just after an eviction and developed in the darkroom of the sublime. None of this is an argument that the valleys are not beautiful, or that their defenders acted in bad faith. It is an observation about what the category was built from, and about what had to be forgotten for the category to feel self-evident.

The construction has practical consequences that its inheritors still manage. If nature is by definition where people are not, then conservation becomes a policy of separation, and the lands most worth protecting are those from which human use is most completely excluded, a logic that has sat more heavily on some populations than others, and that renders invisible the working landscapes, farms, coppices, managed woodlands, where most of the world's people actually meet the natural world. The definition also embarrasses its own flagship preserves. The parks are managed intensively, fires set and suppressed, predators reintroduced by truck, visitor numbers rationed by lottery, so that the experience of the untouched is among the more carefully produced artifacts a modern state offers, a fact that is not a scandal but is, on the definition, a paradox.

At this point a familiar alarm sounds: if wilderness is a construction, has the case for protecting anything been surrendered? The alarm mistakes the argument. To say the concept was assembled from theology, romance, and forgetting is to make a claim about a category, not about the biota. Watersheds filter, wetlands buffer, species exist or go extinct, entirely without reference to how the land is described, and the constructionist point leaves every one of those facts standing. What it removes is a particular justification, protection premised on an emptiness that was manufactured, and a particular baseline, the eviction-era snapshot, treated as the natural state of things. Development interests that quote the critique as a license have read half of it.

What the critique points toward instead is a revaluation of the middle. If the binary of pristine and ruined is the construction's legacy, then the alternative is a continuum of wildness, one on which the managed woodlot, the city river slowly recovering its fish, and the hedgerow between fields all hold value, differing in degree rather than in kind from the celebrated valley. On such a view, conservation's task is not the curation of remnants behind a boundary but the negotiation of human presence everywhere, with the questions shifting accordingly, from whether people belong in nature to which arrangements of people and land can be sustained, and at what cost, and who bears it.

The photograph, in the end, was making an argument, and its frame was the argument's sharpest edge: everything outside it, the parking area, the managed forest, the town whose residents' ancestors burned the meadow, was declared irrelevant to the meaning of what lay inside. Concepts of nature always frame in this way; there is no caption-less view. The choice is not between framed and unframed but between frames examined and frames inherited, and the examination, whatever it costs in innocence, is the beginning of an environmental politics that can say honestly what it is protecting, and from whom, and for whom.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because wilderness is a cultural construction assembled by historians and artists, ecological claims about watersheds and species populations have no objective standing of their own.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Untouched wilderness was assembled from theology, romance, and removal, and seeing this redirects conservation toward a continuum of wildness including inhabited landscapes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: National parks should be closed to visitors, since intensive management makes the experience of untouched nature fraudulent.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The photograph described in the opening paragraph should have included the parking area in order to be considered honest.

explanations.A: This choice commits exactly the misreading the fifth paragraph is built to block. The passage states that watersheds filter, wetlands buffer, and species go extinct entirely without reference to how land is described, and that the constructionist point concerns the category, not the biota, so ecological facts are explicitly left standing.
explanations.B: Correct. The passage traces the concept's assembly from a theology of the sublime, frontier romance, and the eviction that manufactured emptiness, rebuts the inference that construction surrenders the case for protection, and concludes by proposing a continuum of wildness on which managed and inhabited landscapes hold value, shifting conservation's question from whether people belong in nature to which arrangements can be sustained.
explanations.C: This choice converts the fourth paragraph's paradox into a prescription the passage never issues. The intensive management of parks is offered as a paradox for the definition of the untouched, and the passage says explicitly that this is not a scandal; closure of parks is nowhere proposed.
explanations.D: This choice trivializes the closing paragraph's point about frames. The passage argues there is no caption-less view and that all concepts of nature frame; the remedy proposed is the examination of inherited frames, not a compositional rule requiring parking areas in photographs.

questions.stem: The primary function of the fifth paragraph is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: concede that the constructionist critique, taken to its logical conclusion, leaves no principled grounds for protecting any landscape at all.

choices.label: B
choices.text: argue that development interests have understood the critique more accurately than conservationists have.

choices.label: C
choices.text: introduce the historical reversal in the meaning of the word wilderness.

choices.label: D
choices.text: distinguish the critique's claim about a category from claims about biotic reality, blocking the inference that constructedness licenses development.

explanations.A: This choice states the alarm the paragraph is written to answer, not the answer it gives. The paragraph says the alarm mistakes the argument, and specifies that ecological facts stand regardless of how land is described, so no concession of the kind described occurs.
explanations.B: This choice inverts the paragraph's closing judgment. Development interests that quote the critique as a license are said to have read half of it, an accusation of misreading, not a credit for superior understanding.
explanations.C: This choice assigns the paragraph the second paragraph's work. The historical reversal from dread to reverence is traced early in the passage; the fifth paragraph presupposes that history and addresses what follows from it.
explanations.D: Correct. The paragraph separates two kinds of claim, about the category wilderness and about watersheds, wetlands, and species, holds that the critique touches only the first, and concludes that what is removed is a particular justification and baseline rather than the case for protection, which directly blocks the inference that constructedness licenses development.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the pristine-versus-ruined binary is best described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: supportive, since the binary supplies the clearest available standard for prioritizing conservation funds.

choices.label: B
choices.text: critical, treating it as a legacy of the construction that obscures the value of working and recovering landscapes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: ambivalent, since the passage judges the binary useful for remote parks but actively harmful for cities and suburbs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: unconcerned, since the passage treats questions of categorization as irrelevant to environmental politics.

explanations.A: This choice attributes to the binary a virtue the passage never grants. Far from endorsing it as a funding standard, the fourth paragraph argues that the definition renders working landscapes invisible and sits unevenly on different populations, and the sixth paragraph proposes replacing the binary outright.
explanations.B: Correct. The sixth paragraph names the binary of pristine and ruined as the construction's legacy and sets against it a continuum of wildness on which the managed woodlot, the recovering city river, and the hedgerow hold value differing in degree rather than kind, while the fourth paragraph faults the binary's logic for erasing the landscapes where most people meet the natural world.
explanations.C: This choice invents a split verdict the passage does not deliver. No paragraph endorses the binary for parks; the fourth paragraph's discussion of parks presents the binary as generating a paradox even on its flagship terrain.
explanations.D: This choice contradicts the passage's closing argument, which holds that frames and categories shape what environmental politics can honestly say it is protecting; categorization is treated as central to that politics, not irrelevant.

questions.stem: The phrase developed in the darkroom of the sublime, as used in the third paragraph, most nearly conveys that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: early photographs of protected valleys required long exposure times that distorted their subjects.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the theology of the sublime was abandoned once photography made celebrated landscapes widely visible to distant audiences.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the post-eviction land was processed through a reverential aesthetic that presented the snapshot as an original state.

choices.label: D
choices.text: conservation baselines should be established using photographic archives rather than written records.

explanations.A: This choice literalizes the darkroom into a technical claim about exposure times. The sentence uses photographic processing as a figure for conceptual processing; no assertion about camera technology or optical distortion is being made.
explanations.B: This choice reverses the relationship the figure asserts. The sublime is the medium in which the snapshot was developed, an active shaping force, not a doctrine displaced by photography's spread.
explanations.C: Correct. The paragraph states that the baseline treated as nature was in fact a snapshot taken just after an eviction; saying it was developed in the darkroom of the sublime conveys that this post-removal condition was processed through the reverential aesthetic of the second paragraph, emerging as an image of original creation rather than of recent dispossession.
explanations.D: This choice converts a diagnostic metaphor into a methodological recommendation the passage never makes. The figure criticizes how a baseline was produced; it proposes no protocol for producing better ones from archives of any kind.

questions.stem: A regional land trust announces it will fund only the acquisition of tracts rated free of human alteration, excluding from consideration a river corridor through farmland where water quality, fish populations, and bird diversity have been improving for two decades under a cooperative agreement among the farmers. Based on the passage, the author would most likely say the trust's policy:

choices.label: A
choices.text: enacts the separation logic of the constructed category, overlooking a recovering landscape whose value differs in degree, not kind, from preserves.

choices.label: B
choices.text: correctly applies the constructionist critique by refusing to treat any human-altered land as natural, whatever its ecological condition.

choices.label: C
choices.text: is justified because ecological facts such as fish populations have no standing once wilderness is recognized as a construction.

choices.label: D
choices.text: errs only in relying on ratings, since the passage argues that no assessment of human alteration is ever possible.

explanations.A: Correct. The trust's free-of-alteration criterion reproduces the definition by which nature is where people are not, the separation logic the fourth paragraph criticizes for rendering working landscapes invisible, and the excluded corridor, a managed landscape recovering its fish and birds, is precisely the kind of middle case the sixth paragraph's continuum of wildness is designed to bring back into view.
explanations.B: This choice inverts the critique it invokes. The constructionist argument targets the purity criterion itself as manufactured; a policy that hardens that criterion into a funding rule applies the construction, not the critique of it.
explanations.C: This choice repeats the misreading the fifth paragraph explicitly rejects. Fish populations and water quality are exactly the biotic facts the passage says stand entirely without reference to how land is described, so the construction of the category cannot dissolve their standing.
explanations.D: This choice locates the error in measurement rather than in the criterion being measured. The passage nowhere denies that human alteration can be assessed; its argument is that alteration-free status is the wrong test of value, since the continuum view assigns worth to altered, recovering, and managed lands.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings would most strengthen the third paragraph's claim that the pristine baseline was a snapshot taken just after an eviction rather than a state of nature?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that visitor numbers at celebrated preserves have risen steadily since their establishment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that the theology of the sublime was preached most influentially in prosperous cities located far from the remote landscapes it celebrated.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Convergent evidence that celebrated open meadows were produced by centuries of indigenous burning that ceased with the removal of its practitioners.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence that early park photographers earned more from images without human figures than from images with them.

explanations.A: This choice tracks the popularity of preserves after establishment, which says nothing about whether the condition enshrined as the baseline was natural or the recent product of human management interrupted by removal.
explanations.B: This choice concerns where the sublime's audience lived, an interesting sociological fact about the reverence current, but it does not bear on the physical history of the celebrated landscapes or on what produced their condition at the moment of protection.
explanations.C: Correct. The claim is that the baseline mistaken for original nature was actually a post-eviction condition. Convergent physical and documentary evidence that the celebrated open structure was created by indigenous burning, ceased with removal, and then began closing over ties the admired landscape directly to the evicted inhabitants' management, establishing that the snapshot recorded an interrupted human practice rather than a state of nature.
explanations.D: This choice explains a market incentive for empty frames, which supports the opening paragraph's point about removal from the photograph, but it does not address whether the land's celebrated condition was itself the product of the removed inhabitants' practices.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the second paragraph's claim that the reversal in the meaning of wilderness occurred late and quickly, within roughly a century?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that the word wilderness appears with negative connotations in many early religious texts.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that calendar photographs of empty landscapes became commercially popular only in recent decades.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that some modern visitors to protected valleys report feelings of dread rather than reverence when confronting the celebrated scenery.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Texts spanning centuries before the identified period consistently celebrating uncultivated lands as sites of restoration and divine presence.

explanations.A: This choice confirms rather than challenges the paragraph's account, which itself asserts that wilderness long named something dreaded. Early negative connotations are the before state the reversal narrative presupposes.
explanations.B: This choice dates a commercial genre of imagery, not the semantic and attitudinal reversal itself. The reverence could have arisen when the passage says it did whether or not calendar photography monetized it much later.
explanations.C: This choice reports present-day variation in visitor feeling, which is compatible with the historical claim; a reversal in a culture's dominant valuation does not require unanimity of individual response afterward.
explanations.D: Correct. The paragraph's claim is temporal: dread was the long norm and reverence arrived late and fast, fed by the sublime and the frontier romance. A continuous multi-century record of celebrating uncultivated land as restorative and divine would show reverence long predating the identified currents, contradicting both the lateness and the rapidity of the claimed reversal.
